segments.s414-g33: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing what is wanted/anticipated (here closer to 'that'/'so that'). بینم is a first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may I see' or 'I may see' (expressing desire). پسندیده is an adjectival/past-participial form 'pleasing, approved' modifying چهرِ-ترا (چهر + 2nd-person suffix ت + object marker را), so the phrase literally 'that I may see your pleasing face.'
segments.s414-g34: This is a nominal series linked to the previous object (ترا…را is resumed): بزرگی 'greatness' and مهر 'love/favour' are straightforward nouns joined by و; گردی here stands between them as a coordinating noun meaning a kind of renown/glory (an older/poetic sense not fully captured in the provided senses). The final ترا + را again marks these as objects belonging to 'you.'
segments.s414-g35: نشینیم is a first-person plural subjunctive/hortative form meaning 'let us sit' or 'we should sit' (a suggestion/optative). یک با دگر is an idiomatic, slightly archaic phrase 'one with another' = 'together,' with با governing دگر. شادکام is an adjective 'joyful/fortunate' describing the state of those who sit.
segments.s414-g36: به یاد here means 'in the memory/remembrance of'; شهنشاه is the addressee 'the king/emperor' in construct; گیریم is first-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'let us take' or simple present 'we take' used hortatively, and جام is the direct object 'cup' (i.e. 'let us take a cup in memory of the shah').
segments.s414-g37: کنون 'now' sets the time. آنچ is a relative/indefinite compound 'that which/what.' جستم is the past of 'seek' (here 'I sought'); همه یافتم combines همه 'all' with یافتم 'I found,' so 'I have found all that I sought.' Word order is subject-less (typical Persian), with verbs carrying person and tense.
segments.s414-g38: به + خواهشگری means 'by/with entreaty' (خواهشگری = 'entreaty' here, an instrumental phrase). تیز is an adverbial use 'swift/keen' and بشتافتم is the past 'I hastened' or 'I hurried,' so the clause literally reads 'with entreaty, swift, I hastened.'
segments.s414-g39: به پیش تو 'before you / in your presence' uses پیش in the sense 'in the presence of' and تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. آیم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/imperative-ish 'I come' or 'let me come' used here as a simple statement 'I come' and کنون 'now' gives time; بی‌سپاه means 'without troops/unescorted.'
segments.s414-g40: ز تو بشنوم is a desiderative/subjunctive phrasing 'that I may hear from you' (ز = 'from'). بشنوم is first-person singular subjunctive 'may I hear' (a polite/wishful request). هرچ 'whatever' introduces the content clause, فرمود is a past-tense/reporting verb 'he commanded/said,' and شاه names the speaker 'the king' (i.e. 'whatever the king commanded').
segments.s414-g105: به + verbal noun + کنم is a light-verb construction: 'به پوزش کنم' = 'I make/offer an apology.' کنَم is 1st-person present (subjunctive/optative form often used for future or intention in poetry) meaning 'I will / I shall.' نرم modifies خشم ('softly/gently soothe the anger'), and ورا is an enclitic meaning 'toward him / to him' that attaches to the whole phrase.
segments.s414-g106: ببوسم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I will kiss' (used for a resolved intent). The sequence سر و پای و چشم lists the things kissed; Persian uses و between coordinate nouns. The final ورا 'to him' governs all the preceding nouns, so the whole phrase means 'I will kiss his head, feet, and eyes.'
segments.s414-g107: بپرسم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used to express intention ('I will ask'). ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'of.' The nouns/adjectives 'بیدار شاه بلند' form a descriptive phrase 'the awakened, lofty king' (adjectives sandwiching or following the noun is common in poetry; بلند here is an epithet 'lofty').
segments.s414-g108: که introduces the reported question/clause ('why ...'). پایم 'my foot' is the affected noun; چرا 'why' is the interrogative placed before the verb, and کرد is the past tense 'he did/made.' باید + به بند expresses the resulting necessity/state: 'it was necessary (that) into fetters' — together rendering 'why he made my foot have to be in fetters' (i.e. why he bound my foot). Word order reflects poetic inversion but follows Persian syntax: interrogative + verb + predicate.
segments.s414-g109: همه هرچ = 'everything/whatever' (intensifier + indefinite). گفتم is the past tense 'I said / I told' (the subject 'I' is omitted as usual). ترا here is the clitic for 'to you' (indirect object). یاد دار is an idiomatic two-word instruction meaning 'hold/keep in memory' — یاد (memory) + دار (hold/keep); together imperative/commanding 'remember / keep it in mind.'
segments.s414-g110: بگویش is بگو + ش (the enclitic sh = 'it/to him') meaning 'tell it to him' (here: 'tell him'). به marks the indirect object recipient. پرمایه is an epithet meaning 'full-bodied / substantial / mighty' describing اسفندیار; اسفندیار is the proper name (Esfandiyar).
segments.s414-g57: ازان سان که is a fixed subordinating phrase meaning "in that manner / so that," introducing a clause; the clause that follows contains the agent من ("I") and the direct object گردن ژنده پیل ("the mangy elephant's neck"). Note the verb is postponed to the next segment, so this segment functions as the fronted clause/object of that verb.
segments.s414-g58: ببستم is an archaic/prefixed past form meaning "I tied / I bound" (classical b- prefix); it takes گردن ژنده پیل as its object. فکندم is a simple past 'I threw / cast' and here takes implicit the same object; به + دریای نیل marks direction/goal "into the Nile." The two verbs are reported in sequence: I bound (it), then I threw (it) into the Nile.
segments.s414-g59: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction "when/if." از من گناهی is a genitival phrase "a sin from me / a sin of mine," functioning as the theme of بیابد. بیابد is an older 3rd-person-present/optative-form meaning "is found / comes to light," and پدید is a predicative adverb/verb meaning "appears / manifests."
segments.s414-g60: ازان پس is a temporal phrase "after that / thereafter." سر من is the accusative phrase "my head." بباید is a modal verb meaning "must / ought to," and برید here expresses the action "be cut off / to be severed" (classical verbal form). The construction gives an obligation: if the previous clause holds, then my head must be cut.
segments.s414-g61: سخنهای ناخوش is a noun phrase "unpleasant words/speeches." ز من marks source/possession "from me / away from me." دور + دار is an imperative construction meaning "keep (them) distant / keep away." In context دار is an imperative from داشتن/داشتن-like usage: "keep X away from me."
segments.s414-g62: به بدها is a dative/prepositional phrase "toward evils / to bad things." دل دیو رنجور is a noun phrase "the demon's ailing/sore heart." دار again is an imperative "keep / let be" — here: cause or allow the demon's heart to be troubled/ill-disposed with respect to bad things. Word order is topical (prepositional phrase first, then the noun phrase, closing with the imperative verb).
segments.s414-g63: مگوی is a negative imperative "do not say / do not tell." آنچ functions as a relative pronoun "that which." هرگز نگفتست کس is a relative clause "which no one ever said," with نگفتست an old perfect/past form "did not say." The whole segment is the object of the prohibition: "Do not say what no one has ever said."
segments.s414-g64: به مردی مکن is an admonitory formula: به مردی can be vocative/qualificational ("O man / as befits a man") and مکن is the negative imperative "do not do." باد را در قفس places the object (باد را "the wind") inside the locative phrase در قفس "in a cage." The normal order in prose would be باد را در قفس مکن; here word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s414-g97: بخواه is an imperative 'ask/request' (2nd-person). آنچ خواهی is a relative phrase 'that which you want' (آنچ = 'that which'; خواهی = 2nd-person present/root meaning 'you want'). و دیگر ببخش here is the verb phrase 'and (do) give/grant nothing else' — note that the negative particle that negates ببخش comes in the next segment (مکن), so ببخش is the positive imperative root 'grant' which will be turned negative by the following مکن.
segments.s414-g98: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not' which negates the preceding verb (ببخش) — classical Persian often separates the negator. بر دل ما = 'upon our heart' (بر = on/upon; دل = heart; ما = our/us). چنین روز دخش = 'such a day of radiance/splendour' (چنین = such; روز = day; دخش = radiance/splendour) and functions as the object of the prohibition.
segments.s414-g99: درم ده is an imperative request 'give dirhams/pay the money' (درم = coin, ده = give). سپه را uses the definite/object marker را to mark 'the army' as the direct object ('to the army' / 'the army (its pay)'). و تندی مکن joins a second clause with 'and' and the prohibitive مکن: تندی here means 'fierceness/violence' (so 'do not be violent').
segments.s414-g100: چو = 'when'. خوبی بیابی is a verbal clause 'you find/receive goodness/kindness' (بیابی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of یافتن/یافتن used as 'you find'). نژندی مکن is another prohibitive: نژندی = 'baseness/disgrace' and مکن negates it ('do not act basely / do not repay with disgrace').
segments.s414-g101: چو = 'when'. هنگام رفتن = 'the time of going/departure' (هنگام = time; رفتن = to go). فراز آیدت is an idiomatic sequence 'to go up / to ascend' expressed as 'fraz (on high) comes to you' — آیدت is 'it comes to you' and makes the whole phrase temporal ('when the moment to go up arrives for you').
segments.s414-g102: به دیدار خسرو = 'to/for a meeting with Khosrow' (به = to/for; دیدار = meeting/visiting; خسرو = the king's name/title). نیاز آیدت = 'a need/longing will come to you' (نیاز here = longing/need; آیدت = 'comes to you'), so the clause says you will feel compelled/longing to see the king.
segments.s414-g103: عنان با عنان تو بندم is a compact clause: عنان = 'rein'; با = 'with'; عنان تو = 'your rein'; بندم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative 'I bind/tie' (classical poetic form). به راه = 'on the road' or 'along the way'. The sequence expresses 'I tie my rein to yours and set out along the road.'
segments.s414-g104: خرامان بیایم is an optative/hortative 'may I come / I come with a proud, stately gait' (خرامان = adverb 'proudly/stately'; بیایم is 1st-person form often used as 'I come' or 'may I come'). به نزدیک شاه = 'to near the king' (به = to; نزدیک = near; شاه = king), completing the motion toward the sovereign.
segments.s414-g25: A stacked list of abstract qualities introduced by the preposition به (here instrumental/qualitative: 'by/with'). Each noun is connected by و (and). مردی is an abstract noun meaning 'manliness/gentlemanliness'; رای here means 'plan/counsel'; خرد means 'wisdom'. گردی is a poetic/archaic noun in this context paired with مردی to mean a kind of greatness or stature.
segments.s414-g26: همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic particle that gives a habitual or emphatic sense and often marks present/future narrative ('will' / 'keeps on'). بر + گذرد (here گذر/بگذشتن) means 'to pass over/above' and idiomatically 'to surpass' or 'to excel beyond'. نیاکانِ خود is the object ('his own ancestors').
segments.s414-g27: پدیدست is an impersonal/third‑person verb 'it appears / becomes manifest'. نامت is 'your name' (here meaning 'your reputation' or 'fame'). به هندوستان indicates the location ('in India') — به is used in the sense 'in' for geographic scope.
segments.s414-g28: A continuation of the locative list: repeated به + place names. Each به here likewise indicates the domain/place ('in Rome', 'in China', 'in the land of magic/sorcery') and و links the items in the list.
segments.s414-g29: ازین is the fused form of از + این 'from these'; here it introduces the source ('from these counsels'). داشتم is the past tense 'I had' and takes سپاس ('thanks/gratitude') as its object; من is the subject pronoun for emphasis.
segments.s414-g30: نیایش کنَم uses the verb کردن in the sense 'to perform/offer' and here with the noun نیایش ('prayer/supplication') yields 'I would pray' or 'I used to offer prayer'; the present/subjunctive form کنم is used in an habitual/optative sense. روز و شب is an adverbial pair 'day and night', and در سه‌پاس adverbially qualifies the manner as 'in threefold fashion' (three‑step / triple refrain — a ritual descriptor).
segments.s414-g31: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning 'from'. یزدان is 'God' or 'the divine'. همی again gives an ongoing/emphatic sense ('continually/always'). آرزو is the object 'wish/desire' and خواستم is the speaker's past tense 'I wished/asked/prayed'. Together they mean 'I prayed to God, continuously desiring'.
segments.s414-g32: که introduces a content/result clause ('that ...'). اکنون 'now'; بتو 'to you' (dative). دل here means 'heart' (object of the hoped‑for action). بیاراستم is a first‑person form of بیاراستن 'to adorn/prepare/fit' used optatively/subjunctively after آرزو خواستم — 'that I might now adorn (win) my heart to you' i.e. 'that I might win your heart now.'
segments.s414-g9: چو is a conditional particle here meaning 'if' (classical/poetic use). The segment lists qualities/possessions in a row (مردی, پیروزی, خواسته) joined by و 'and'; Persian allows a nominal series before the verb. مردی here is an abstract noun ('manliness' or valor) rather than 'a man'.
segments.s414-g10: ورا 'to/for him' precedes the verb باشد; classical Persian often uses باشد (present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to be') to state existence or possession ('be to him' = 'he has' / 'is given to him'). آراسته is a past-participial adjective following the noun گنج, so گنج آراسته = 'treasure adorned/laid out'.
segments.s414-g11: This is a coordinated nominal phrase: بزرگی 'greatness' then گردی and نام بلند 'a lofty name'. The nouns are linked by و as a simple list; بلند modifies نام and appears after it as the usual adjective position. The exact sense of گردی is uncertain in the provided options, so it is treated as a lexical item not matched to the given senses.
segments.s414-g12: به نزد is a prepositional expression meaning 'with/among' or 'in the presence of'; گرانمایگان ارجمند is a compound epithet (گرانمایگان 'illustrious/important people', ارجمند 'esteemed') serving as the group that values the listed qualities.
segments.s414-g13: به گیتی 'in the world' is a locative phrase. بدین سان is a fixed poetic expression meaning 'in this manner' (here represented by the split parts ب + دین + سان; دین as separated here does not carry its ordinary sense and functions within the fused expression بدین). که اکنون تویی is a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that you are now' (تویی = 'you are').
segments.s414-g14: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'one ought not' / 'you should not'; داری is the present second-person singular of داشتن 'to have' (here 'to carry/hold' in combination with سر), and سر بدخویی is a collocation meaning 'a head/temper of ill-nature' (i.e. 'to be ill-tempered'). The object/headnoun follows the verb in normal Persian order.
segments.s414-g15: بباشیم is a first-person plural jussive/imperative 'let us be' (classical/polite exhortation). بر داد literally 'on/for justice' here means 'be just' or 'stand for justice' (بر + noun). یزدان‌پرست is a compound noun 'God-worshipper' used adjectivally/predicatively 'and (be) God-fearing/pious.'
segments.s414-g16: نگیریم is the negative jussive/subjunctive 'let us not take' (ن- negation + گیر + ایم first-person-plural ending). دست بدی را is the direct object marked by را ('the hand of evil' / 'evil's hand'), and به دست literally 'into/by the hand' repeats دست to form the idiom meaning 'to take by the hand' i.e. 'to embrace/join with evil'; Persian uses را after the object noun to mark definiteness.
segments.s414-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past, 3rd-person singular verb 'he heard' placed before the subject (poetic word order: verb then subject: "بشنید رستم"). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source (بهمن), and سخن is the object 'speech/words' that he heard.
segments.s414-g2: پراندیشه شد is a copular construction: the adjective پراندیشه 'thoughtful/pensive' plus شد 'became' gives 'became pensive'. The grammatical subject follows (مغز مرد کهن — 'the mind of the old man/hero'), a common inverted order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s414-g3: چنین گفت literally 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past of 'to say' used here as reporting verb). کآری is an archaic/poetic particle (here introducing 'indeed' or the matter being reported) and شنیدم is first-person past 'I heard', with پیام as its direct object 'the message/tidings'. Note some of these words are archaic or compressed in poetic usage.
segments.s414-g4: دلم is the possessed form 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م). شد is 'became' and governs the predicate شادکام 'joyful/fortunate'. به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'at/at the sight of you' (literally 'to your meeting'), placed before the adjective for emphasis — typical poetic ordering.
segments.s414-g5: ز من 'from me' marks the source/owner of the reply. پاسخ این 'this reply' is the thing being sent; بر and به both function as directional/prepositional markers here (poetic redundancy and inverted order), so the phrase culminates with به اسفندیار 'to Esfandiyar' as the intended recipient. Poetic word order places these prepositional pieces around the noun.
segments.s414-g6: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative particle here (the 'ای' vocative attached in a fused form) used to call out to someone: 'O ...'. The following words are epithets: شیردل 'lion‑hearted', مهتر 'chief/leader', نامدار 'renowned' — a string of vocative titles addressing Esfandiyar.
segments.s414-g7: هرانکس که is a free relative 'whoever' + relative particle. دارد is the present tense 'has' (generic/prescriptive sense), روانش is 'his soul/mind' with the possessive suffix -ش, and خرد is the noun 'wisdom' functioning as the possessed quality: 'whoever has wisdom in his mind.'
segments.s414-g8: سر مایه is a compound 'chief/source' or 'principal resource' and کارها 'the affairs/things' is its object. بنگرد is the 3rd‑person subjunctive/jussive of نگر ('to look/consider') with the verbal prefix ب‑ found in classical Persian subjunctives; here it carries the meaning 'should look at / should consider.'
segments.s414-g49: چو introduces a subordinate conditional clause here ('if/when'). پاداش آن رنج بند آیدم is a compact phrase meaning 'the recompense/reward of that suffering comes to me'; آیدم is 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'may/does come to me' used impersonally about something 'befalling' the speaker. بند is used as a nominal element attached to رنج ('bond/linked hardship' or part of a poetic compound) rather than the stanza sense.
segments.s414-g50: که here introduces an explanatory or appositive clause ('that/namely'), and the clause states the ill that 'comes to me': از indicates origin/source ('from'), شاه ایران is the agent/source (the King of Iran), گزند آیدم uses گزند 'harm' as the thing that 'comes to me' with آیدم again 'comes to me / befalls me.'
segments.s414-g51: همان به که is a fixed idiom meaning roughly 'it is better that' or 'better that...', with همان functioning as a particle setting the evaluative tone. گیتی is 'the world', and نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive/optative third-person form 'should not see' or 'not see'; کسی here means 'anyone/no one' (in negative contexts 'no one').
segments.s414-g52: چو is temporal/conditional 'when/if'; بیند is third-person subjunctive/present of 'see' (he/one sees). بدو = 'to him' (dative pronoun). در here means 'in/there' (i.e. in that world/there), and نماند بسی is a negative construction: نماند 'will not remain/be left' + بسی 'much/enough' — 'much will not remain for him.'
segments.s414-g53: بیابم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative form of 'یافتن' (literally 'may I find' / 'if I find' — marking a hoped-for or conditional action). بگویم here is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of 'to say' ('I will say' / 'let me say'); together the two verbs form a protasis-result relation ('if I find, I will say'). همه راز خویش = 'all my secrets' where خویش is expressed by the noun خوی + enclitic ش (his/my/own).
segments.s414-g54: ز (poetic variant of از) means 'from' or 'toward' in a broad, poetic sense; گیتی = 'the world'. برافرازم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of 'to raise up' ('I will exalt/raise'), and آواز خویش = 'my voice/song' (خوی + ش again forming the possessive). The two halves connect by coordination: after saying his secrets he will lift his voice to the world.
segments.s414-g55: به بازو = 'on/to the arm' (به indicating direction/attachment). ببندم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of بستن 'to bind/fasten' ('I will bind' / 'let me bind'), یکی پالهنگ = 'a certain leopard' or 'one leopard' (یکی used as 'a certain' or 'one'). The phrase expresses the speaker's planned act of fastening a leopard (or a leopard-skin) to his arm.
segments.s414-g56: بیاویز is an imperative/subjunctive form of 'آویختن' meaning 'I will hang' / 'let me hang'. پایم = 'my foot' (پای + enclitic ـم). به marks attachment/location 'to/with', چرم = 'leather/skin', and پلنگ = 'leopard' (so 'leopard-skin leather'). The line describes hanging/fastening his foot with leopard-skin (i.e., wearing leopard leather).
segments.s414-g41: بیارم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive form used here with a future/habitual sense: “I bring” / “I will bring.” برت (contracted بر + تو) is an object/adverbial meaning “to you” or “upon you.” عهد شاهان داد is a compact noun phrase where عهد “covenant/oath” is modified by شاهان (genitive, “of the kings”) and the final داد functions as a verbal adjective/past verb meaning “gave” (literally “the covenant that kings gave”). The whole segment thus means “I will bring to you the covenant that the kings gave.”
segments.s414-g42: ز introduces a source or starting point: “from.” کیخسرو آغاز is an ablative/time phrase meaning “the beginning/time of Kay Khosrow” (آغاز = beginning). تا is the standard preposition “until/up to,” and کیقباد is the terminal name “Kay Qobad.” Together the segment gives the chronological span of the covenant: “from Kay Khosrow’s beginning to Kay Qobad.”
segments.s414-g43: کنون is an adverb meaning “now.” شهریارا is the vocative form addressing the king (“O Shahryar/O king”). تو is the personal pronoun “you” (emphatic here because the speaker addresses the king directly). در کار من is a prepositional phrase “in my case/affair” (در = in; کار = affair/cause; من = me/my). The whole segment is an address: “Now, O king, you (look) at my affair.”
segments.s414-g44: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (a noun/verb root meaning “look/keep watch”) plus کن (imperative ‘do’), producing “look!” or “pay attention!” به introduces the objects of attention “to/at”; کردار و آزار من lists those objects — کردار “conduct/deeds” and آزار من “my harm/suffering” (و = and; من = me/my). So the clause orders the king to regard the speaker’s behavior and the injuries suffered by him.
segments.s414-g45: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” آن نیکویها is the topic “those good/virtuous things” (آن = those; نیکویها = plural of ‘good/virtuous’ implying deeds or acts). که من کرده‌ام is a relative clause “which I have done” (که = that/which; کرده‌ام = perfect 1st singular “I have done”). The clause sets up a conditional: “If those good deeds which I have done…”
segments.s414-g46: همان here means “the same” (repeating or insisting on equality). رنجهایی “the hardships/sufferings” is the noun being asserted as equal; که من برده‌ام is another relative clause “which I have borne/experienced” (برده‌ام = perfect ‘I have borne/ carried/undergone’). The segment expresses: “(give me) the same hardships that I have borne.” Note the sentence is elliptical (the verb implying grant/reward is omitted but understood).
segments.s414-g47: پرستیدن is an infinitive/noun-like verbal form meaning “to worship/serve.” شهریاران is the plural “kings/rulers” and functions as the object of پرستیدن. همان follows as a predicate meaning “is the same” or “unchanged.” The phrase states a generalization: “The worship/service of kings is the same.”
segments.s414-g48: از امروز تا روز پیشی is a temporal span “from today until the previous/former day” (از = from; امروز = today; تا = until; روز = day; پیشی here = precedence/former). The final همان reiterates “the same.” Together the segment emphasizes that this sameness holds across time: “from today back to earlier days it is the same.”
segments.s414-g73: تو (address) + آن کن is an imperative phrase: تو is the vocative/subject ‘you’ (poetic), آن 'that' is the demonstrative object and کن is the imperative 'do'. The following که introduces a relative clause ('that which...') and از پادشاهان سزاست is the clause 'is fitting of kings' completing 'that'. Note the classical word order: demonstrative + imperative followed by a relative clause explaining what to do.
segments.s414-g74: مدار is used poetically as a nominal/abstract 'sphere/realm' (i.e. keep within the proper sphere). آز را is the definite object 'greed' marked by را. دیو is an apposition (the demon = Greed), and بر دست راست is a locative phrase 'on the right hand'. The whole chunk says essentially 'keep Greed, the demon, in/on the right hand (i.e. at your right side/control)'.
segments.s414-g75: به مردی is an adverbial phrase 'as befits a man / in the manner of a man'. ز + دل marks the source 'from the heart' and دور کن is an imperative 'remove / put far away' (دور here means 'away/removed' and is used with the imperative کن). خشم و کین are the coordinated objects 'anger and hatred'.
segments.s414-g76: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). به چشم جوانی is a prepositional phrase 'with the eye/sight of youth' (به + noun). مبین is an imperative-like poetic form meaning 'see / behold' (here used to command the addressee to view the world through youthful eyes); this is an archaic/poetic usage rather than the literal adjective senses listed for مبین.
segments.s414-g77: به دل خرمی دار is an imperative request: به دل 'in the heart', خرمی 'joy/delight', and دار here functions as an imperative 'have/keep' (classical/poetic usage of دار = 'hold/keep'). و بگذر ز رود is a second imperative clause: بگذر 'pass on / go by' with ز رود 'from the river / beyond the stream' marking what to pass by.
segments.s414-g78: ترا باد is an optative formula: ترا (to you) is formed from the 2nd‑person suffix ت plus را, and باد here is the optative particle meaning 'may it be'. از پاک یزدان درود is the wished thing 'blessing/greeting from the pure God' (از = from). The whole is 'May blessing from the pure God be to you.'
segments.s414-g79: گرامی کن is an imperative 'honor / hold dear' (گرامی used as a verb phrase + کن imperative). ایوان ما را is the object 'our portico/hall' with ما 'our' and را marking the definite object. به سور indicates the means 'with a feast / by a banquet' (به + سور).
segments.s414-g80: مباش is the prohibitive imperative 'do not be'. از پرستنده خویش دور is a locative/complement phrase 'far from (thy) worshipper/devotee': از = 'from', پرستنده = 'worshipper/devotee', خویش + ش is the possessive formation ('own / his/your own' — here referring back to the addressee's devotee), and دور = 'far/distant'. The clause therefore prohibits being distant from one’s worshipper/devotee.
segments.s414-g65: بزرگان (subject) + به دانش (instrumental adjunct) + بیابند (3rd‑person plural present/general) + راه (direct object). Here به is instrumental ‘by/through’ and بیابند is a general, timeless present ‘they find/attain’; راه is the thing found (‘a way/method’). Word order is flexible but the clause reads literally ‘great ones by knowledge find a way.’
segments.s414-g66: ز دریا گذر نیست is an existential negative: ز (poetic ‘from/of’) + دریا ‘sea’ + گذر ‘passage’ + نیست ‘does not exist/there is no…’. بی‑آشناه stands as an omitted‑agent adjective ‘for the unfamiliar’ (the person unfamiliar with the sea). The whole means literally ‘From the sea a passage is not (for) the unfamiliar.’
segments.s414-g67: همان تابش مهر is a noun phrase ‘that same radiance of love/affection.’ نتوان is the negative modal ‘cannot’ and نهفت is a past participle/infinitive used predicatively ‘be hidden/concealed.’ Together نتوان نهفت = ‘cannot be hidden.’
segments.s414-g68: نه introduces a second negative clause (‘nor’). روبه here is the animal ‘fox’ as the patient/object, توان کرد is a periphrastic ability construction (توان auxiliary + کرد ‘do/make’) meaning ‘can make’ or here in negative ‘cannot make.’ با شیر جفت is a prepositional phrase ‘a mate for/with the lion.’ The clause literally: ‘nor can (one) make the fox a mate with the lion.’
segments.s414-g69: تو is the vocative/subject ‘you.’ بر راه من is a prepositional phrase ‘upon my way/path’ (بر = on/upon). بر ستیزه مریز is negative imperative: مریز (lit. ‘do not pour/spill’) governs بر ستیزه ‘upon/into strife’ — idiomatically ‘do not bring/raise strife against my path.’
segments.s414-g70: که introduces the reason (‘for/because’). من خود یکی مایه‌ام در ستیز: من خود ‘I myself,’ یکی مایه‌ام is a predicative phrase ‘I am one mâyé’ (مایه here = substance/force/resource) with the possessive suffix ‑ام, and در ستیز ‘in battle/strife’ locates that force. The sense: ‘for I myself am a single decisive force in combat.’
segments.s414-g71: ندیدست کس is an impersonal/perfect construction: ندیدست here functions ‘has not been seen’ (passive/impersonal) and کس is the indefinite agent ‘by anyone/​no one’ (context gives negative meaning). بند بر پای من = ‘a fetter on my foot.’ The clause literally: ‘A fetter on my foot has not been seen by anyone.’
segments.s414-g72: نه (nor) negates the second clause. بگرفت is a past transitive ‘seized/took,’ پیل is the agent ‘elephant,’ and ژیان جای من is a noun grouping ‘(my) place of life/abode’ (ژیان ‘life’ modifying جای ‘place’) — word order is compressed. Taken together: ‘nor did an elephant seize my place.’
segments.s414-g17: سخن هرچ بر گفتنش روی نیست = literal order 'speech whatever / upon its saying / face is-not'. هرچ (an older form of هر چه) means 'whatever' and heads the nominal phrase سخنِ هرچ 'whatever speech'. گفتنش is the masdar/infinitive گفتن with the possessive suffix -ش ('its being said' / 'to say it'). بر + گفتنش functions adverbially 'in/when saying it' or 'to say it'. روی (here 'face') is used metaphorically: روی نیست = 'has no face / is not proper' (i.e. 'not fit to be said'). نیست is the negative copula 'is not'.
segments.s414-g18: درختی بود کش بر و بوی نیست = 'there was a tree / (that) کش بر / and scent is-not'. بود is the past copula 'was'. درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree'. کش here is a verbal/adjectival element 'that can be pulled/cut' (used attributively in poetry) and combines with بر to indicate the action 'to pull/cut down'. و connects the clauses, and بوی نیست states that the tree 'has no scent' (بوی = scent, نیست = 'is not'). Word order is compressed/elliptical in the poem but reads as 'there was a tree that could be cut, and it had no fragrance.'
segments.s414-g19: وگر جان تو بسپرد راه آز = 'if / life of you / (you) entrust / way of greed'. وگر is the conditional 'if'. جان تو (object before verb) is the thing being entrusted. بسپرد is the present/optative or simple verb سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' used in the protasis ('if you entrust'). راهِ آز is a genitive compound 'the way/path of greed' (آز = greed). Word order places the object and possessor before the verb, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s414-g20: شود کار بی‌سود بر تو دراز = 'becomes / work / fruitless / upon you / long'. شود (from شدن) is a verbal auxiliary meaning 'becomes' or 'will be'. کار بی‌سود is the predicate 'a fruitless affair'. بر تو دراز is a prepositional phrase 'long for you' (بر = 'to/for/upon' here, تو = 'you', دراز = 'long/protracted'). The clause means 'the undertaking will prove useless and will be prolonged for you.'
segments.s414-g21: چو مهتر سراید سخن سخته به = 'when / the chief / composes / speech / difficult / (better)'. چو is the temporal/conditional 'when'. مهتر 'chief/elder' is the agent; سراید (شعری/سرودن) is the classical verb 'to compose/sing' used for composing speech or verse. سخنِ سخته is the object: سخن modified by سخته 'difficult/harsh'. The final به begins the comparative construction (به ز … = 'better than …') and therefore attaches syntactically to the next segment.
segments.s414-g22: ز گفتار بد کام پردخته به = 'than / from / speech / bad / desire / well‑wrought / (better)'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' used here in the comparative phrase 'به ز' = 'better than'. گفتار بدِ کام is a genitive phrase 'speech of ill desire' (i.e. speech springing from bad appetite). پردخته is the past participle 'wrought/composed' (well‑wrought); the closing به completes the comparative sense established in the previous segment ('better ... than ...'). The line as a whole contrasts a well‑crafted difficult utterance with speech born of bad desire.
segments.s414-g23: ز گفتارت آن کس بدی بنده شاد = 'from your speech / that person / بدی / servant / glad'. ز گفتارت is a prepositional phrase 'from your speech' (گفتارت = گفتار + possessive -ت). آن کس 'that person' appears as the topicalized noun. بنده شاد functions as a small clause 'the servant (was) glad' with the copula omitted as typical in poetry (بنده = 'servant' here likely a self‑referential or deferential term). بدی sits between کس and بنده and functions adjectivally or as a moral comment modifying کس or as a vocative/epithet; the exact pragmatic role is determined by the surrounding lines but grammatically it is an adjective/noun placed in the cluster.
segments.s414-g24: که گفتی که چون تو ز مادر نزاد = 'that / you said / that / like you / from mother / was born'. The first که introduces reported speech ('that you said'), the second que introduces the quoted content. گفتی here is the verb 'you said' (past tense) introducing the quotation. چون تو 'like you' modifies the implied noun in the quotation, ز مادر 'from a mother' gives origin, and نزاد is a past/formal verbal form meaning 'was born' (classical past/passive sense). Together they report: 'you said that none like you was born of a mother' — i.e. a praise-form utterance. The double که is the classical Persian pattern for 'you said that ...'.
segments.s414-g81: چنان چون is a classical comparative framing, 'چنان چون' = 'as/just as' or 'such as'; it introduces a likeness. بدم here is an archaic predicative form meaning something like 'I am (of low rank/fortune)'; کهتر is an adjective 'lesser/inferior' modifying the implied subject; کیقباد is the proper name Kay Qobad and completes the simile ('inferior to Kay Qobad'). Word order is poetic (comparative phrase fronted).
segments.s414-g82: کنون is an adverb 'now'; از تو is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from you' giving the cause; دارم is first-person present 'I have/hold' used here with the following nouns as its objects (literally 'I have heart and brain glad'); دل و مغز are two coordinated nouns 'heart and mind/brain'; شاد is a predicative adjective following those objects ('happy/joyful'). The whole clause states the speaker's present emotional state caused by 'you'.
segments.s414-g83: چو + آیی = temporal 'when you come'; آیی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/poetic form of 'to come' (addressing 'you'). به ایوان من is the directional phrase 'to my iwan/porch' with the possessor 'من' after the noun (poetic word order). با سپاه 'with an army/host' is appended to indicate accompaniment.
segments.s414-g84: هم‌ایدر is a fused classical adverb 'also here' (i.e., 'here as well'); به شادی 'in/for joy' marks the manner or occasion of staying; بباشی is a 2nd-person present/optative or hortative form of 'بودن' used like an injunction/benediction 'may you be / remain'; دو ماه is a duration 'two months' placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s414-g85: برآساید is a classical optative/3rd-person form of برآسیدن meaning 'may it be eased/relieved' or 'relief will come'; از رنج marks the source/cause 'from the suffering/hardship'; مرد و ستور are coordinated nouns 'men and cattle/beasts' (the things suffering). The verb stands before its complements in elevated/poetic order.
segments.s414-g86: دل دشمنان is a genitive construction 'the hearts of the enemies' (noun + genitive plural). گردد is the classical 3rd-person present/optative of 'گردیدن' meaning 'become/turn' (here 'be turned'); از رشک کور uses از to indicate cause ('from/because of blind envy'), with کور as a postpositive adjective modifying رشک ('blind envy'). The clause expresses the hoped-for effect on enemies.
segments.s414-g87: همه is a universal quantifier 'all'; دشت نخچیر is a genitive-like noun pair 'the plain's game/prey' (poetic noun–noun compound); و مرغ اندر آب adds 'and the birds in the water' with اندر (classical 'in/within') marking location. Word order is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s414-g88: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; دیر here is an adverb 'late/long' ('if you stay too long'); مانی is the 2nd-person present/poetic form 'you remain/stay' (this verbal sense is classical and not listed in the provided sense choices, so marked -1); بگیرد is 3rd-person singular present of 'گرفتن' here 'seize/take hold of', and شتاب is the noun 'haste' that bگیرد governs ('haste will seize [you]'). Poetic word order places the verb before its noun in the final clause.
segments.s414-g89: ببینم is an optative/subjunctive first-person form meaning “may I see” or “let me see.” ز تو is the poetic preposition z (= از) meaning “from you.” زور مردان جنگ is a genitive chain: زور “strength/might” qualifies مردان جنگ “men/warriors of battle,” i.e. “the might of the warriors in battle.” Word order is typical Persian: possessor phrase follows the possessed noun (زورِ مردانِ جنگ).
segments.s414-g90: به here is instrumental/adverbial (‘by/with’). شمشیر is the instrument ‘sword.’ شیر افگنی‌گر پلنگ is a poetic noun phrase in which پلنگ (panther) is qualified by شیر افگنی‌گر, an agentive compound meaning roughly ‘lion‑slaying’ (archaic/poetic formation with -گر). The whole segment describes the panther as slaying lions by the sword.
segments.s414-g91: چو خواهی که is a conditional/subjunctive clause head: چو = “if/when” and خواهی = “you wish/want,” followed by the complementizer که “that.” لشکر به ایران بری is the subordinate clause; بری here is an archaic/poetic subjunctive form meaning ‘you lead/bring/let go’ (cf. modern بَبری/ببری), so the clause means “if you wish that (you) bring the army into Iran.”
segments.s414-g92: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/near” or “close to.” شاه دلیران is a nominal phrase where شاه is head noun and دلیران (pl.) is an epithet/possessive-like attribute “of the brave” (i.e. the brave king or king of heroes). The final بری repeats the archaic subjunctive ‘bring/lead’ directed toward the understood subject “you.”
segments.s414-g93: گشایم is an optative/subjunctive first-person verb meaning “I will open” or “may I open” (poetic form of گشودن/گشودن‑type verbs). در گنجهای کهن is a locative phrase: در “in” + گنجهای (treasures/treasure‑houses) + کهن “ancient,” i.e. “in the old treasure vaults.”
segments.s414-g94: که is the relative/complementizer “which/that.” ایدر (اینجا) = “here.” فکندم is the first‑person past of فکندن and here means “I placed/put” (not merely ‘throw’). به شمشیر بن is a locative phrase: به “at/to” + شمشیر “sword” + بن “base/bottom,” i.e. “which I placed here at the sword’s base/hilt.”
segments.s414-g95: به پیش تو = “before you / in your presence,” with به + پیش used to mark presentation. آرم is a first‑person present/optative verb here meaning “I will bring/present” (poetic; note that this form can be archaic and functions like ‘I place/bring’). همه هرچ هست = “all / whatever exists” (همه = all, هرچ = whatever, هست = exists/is).
segments.s414-g96: کجا here functions as a relative/adverbial “which/where” linking back to هرچ (i.e. “which I gathered”). گرد کردن is a light‑verb compound meaning “to gather/collect” (گرد + کردن), expressed as گرد کردم “I gathered.” به نیروی دست is instrumental — “by the strength/power of (my) hand,” with نیروی = strength and دست = hand (here literal/manual force).

sentences.s414-s17: that I may see your pleasing face — your greatness, your glory, and your love.
sentences.s414-s18: Let us sit together, joyful and content; in memory of the king let us take the cup.
sentences.s414-s19: Now what I sought I have all found; with eager entreaty I hastened swiftly.
sentences.s414-s20: I come now before you without an army; may I hear from you whatever the king has commanded.
sentences.s414-s53: I will apologize and gently soothe his anger, and I will kiss his head, feet, and eyes.
sentences.s414-s54: I will ask the awakened, lofty king why he had to put my foot in fetters.
sentences.s414-s55: Keep in mind everything I told you; tell it to the stalwart Esfandiyar.
sentences.s414-s29: In that way — I bound the mangy elephant's neck and cast it into the Nile.
sentences.s414-s30: If ever a sin is found in me (appears), then afterward my head must be cut off.
sentences.s414-s31: Keep unpleasant words away from me; keep the demon's heart sick toward evils.
sentences.s414-s32: Do not say that which no one ever said; as a man, do not put the wind in a cage.
sentences.s414-s49: Ask for whatever you wish, and give nothing more; do not lay such a day of radiance upon our heart.
sentences.s414-s50: Give the army its pay, and be not violent; when you receive kindness, do not repay it with baseness.
sentences.s414-s51: When the time to depart upwards comes to you, a longing to see Khosrow will come upon you.
sentences.s414-s52: I will bind my rein with your rein on the road and, striding proudly, come near the king.
sentences.s414-s13: By manliness and greatness, by counsel and wisdom, he will surpass his ancestors.
sentences.s414-s14: Your name is made manifest in India, in Rome, in China, and in the land of sorcery.
sentences.s414-s15: I was grateful for these counsels; I would offer supplication day and night in a threefold (ritual).
sentences.s414-s16: From God I continually desired (prayed), that now I might win your heart.
sentences.s414-s5: If a man has manliness, victory and what he desires, and treasure is arranged for him.
sentences.s414-s6: Greatness, renown and a lofty name are valued among honored, illustrious people.
sentences.s414-s7: In the world, in the manner you are now, you ought not to be ill-tempered.
sentences.s414-s8: Let us be just and God-fearing; let us not take evil by the hand.
sentences.s414-s1: When Rostam heard Bahman's words, the old hero's mind grew pensive.
sentences.s414-s2: He said thus: 'I indeed heard the tidings; my heart became joyful at seeing you.'
sentences.s414-s3: Let my reply about this be given to Esfandiyar: 'O lion‑hearted, renowned chief!'
sentences.s414-s4: Whoever has wisdom in his mind should consider the root/source of affairs.
sentences.s414-s25: If the recompense for that toil should come to me — that I be harmed by the King of Iran.
sentences.s414-s26: Better that no one should see the world; when one does see it, little remains for him.
sentences.s414-s27: If I can find (the chance), I will tell all my secrets and lift up my voice to the world.
sentences.s414-s28: I will bind a leopard to my arm; I will hang my foot with leopard-skin leather.
sentences.s414-s21: I will bring you the covenant the kings gave, from the time of Kay Khosrow up to Kay Qobad.
sentences.s414-s22: Now, O Shahryar, consider my case; look at my conduct and the harm done to me.
sentences.s414-s23: If those good deeds that I have done — (give me) the same hardships that I have borne.
sentences.s414-s24: Worshipping (or serving) kings is the same — from today to the former day it is the same.
sentences.s414-s37: Do that which befits kings; keep the demon Greed at your right hand.
sentences.s414-s38: Act like a man: put away anger and hatred from your heart; see the world with the eye of youth.
sentences.s414-s39: Keep joy in your heart and pass beyond the stream; may blessing come to you from the pure God.
sentences.s414-s40: Honor our hall with a feast; do not be far from your worshipper.
sentences.s414-s33: The great find a way through knowledge; for the unfamiliar there is no crossing of the sea.
sentences.s414-s34: That same radiance of love cannot be hidden, nor can a fox be made the mate of a lion.
sentences.s414-s35: You, do not pour (bring) strife upon my way; for I myself am a single force in combat.
sentences.s414-s36: No one has seen fetters on my feet, nor did an elephant seize my place.
sentences.s414-s9: Not every word is fit to be spoken; there was a tree that could be felled, yet it had no scent.
sentences.s414-s10: But if you entrust your life to the path of greed, your undertaking will become fruitless and be prolonged for you.
sentences.s414-s11: When a chief composes a harsh / difficult speech, it is better that it be well‑wrought than that it spring from ill‑willed words.
sentences.s414-s12: By your words that man — the servant — was made glad, for you said that none like you was born of a mother.
sentences.s414-s41: As I am (so) — inferior to Kay Qobad — now because of you my heart and mind are glad.
sentences.s414-s42: When you come to my iwan with your host, stay here too in joy for two months.
sentences.s414-s43: Relief will come from the hardships of men and beasts, and the hearts of enemies will be turned blind with envy.
sentences.s414-s44: All the plain's game and the birds in the water — if you loiter long, haste will seize you.
sentences.s414-s45: Let me see from you the might of the warriors in battle — the panther that slays lions with the sword.
sentences.s414-s46: If you wish to bring the army into Iran, bring it close to the brave king.
sentences.s414-s47: I will open the ancient treasure-vaults which I placed here at the base of the sword.
sentences.s414-s48: I will bring before you whatever there is — everything I gathered by the strength of my hand.
